evidence.question: Is Fox News (Fox News Channel) currently unavailable in Canada, and if so, in what distribution forms (cable/satellite/IPTV/streaming) and since when?
evidence.answer: Evidence indicates Fox News Channel is currently available in Canada via licensed Canadian TV distributors (e.g., Rogers cable/IPTV) and is authorized by the CRTC for distribution as a non-Canadian programming service. The CRTC’s revised list of non-Canadian programming services authorized for distribution includes “Fox News,” indicating it is permitted for carriage in Canada. evidence.question: Does Canada’s Radio Act (or Broadcasting Act) contain provisions that make it a crime to 'lie to the public via airwaves' (i.e., criminalize false or misleading news/broadcast content), and what are the exact sections and enforcement mechanisms?
evidence.answer: Evidence does not support the specific framing that a Canadian “Radio Act” makes it a crime to “lie to the public via airwaves.” The former “Radio Act” has been replaced by the Radiocommunication Act, which is primarily about spectrum/radio apparatus authorization and related offences, not a general criminal prohibition on false news. Separately, Canada’s broadcast regulatory framework (under the Broadcasting Act via CRTC regulations) does include a prohibition on licensees broadcasting “false or misleading news” (e.g., Television Broadcasting Regulations, 1987, s. 5(1)(d)), but that is a licensing/regulatory rule enforced by the CRTC (conditions of licence, compliance measures, and—under the modernized Broadcasting Act—administrative monetary penalties), not a general Criminal Code-style “crime of lying on air.” Historically, the Criminal Code did contain an indictable offence for “spreading false news” (former s. 181), but it was struck down by the Supreme Court of Canada in R. v. Zundel (1992) and later repealed in 2019; this is distinct from the Broadcasting Act/CRTC regime.
evidence.scraped_text: Section 5(1) provides: “A licensee shall not broadcast … (d) any false or misleading news.”

evidence.question: Have Canadian regulators (CRTC) or courts ever cited the Radio Act/Broadcasting Act (or related regulations) as the reason Fox News is not carried/available in Canada, including any formal decisions, denials, or revocations?
evidence.answer: CRTC records show Fox News was authorized for distribution in Canada (added to the eligible digital satellite services lists) in a 2004 CRTC public notice, undermining the premise that it is “not carried/available” due to Canadian law. In that 2004 decision, the CRTC’s analysis focuses on competitiveness with Canadian services and whether special carriage conditions (like those applied to Al Jazeera) were warranted; it explicitly concludes there was no record-based cause for concern requiring special conditions and approves adding Fox News to the digital lists. Separately, AFP’s fact check (citing CRTC documentation) states the CRTC approved Fox News distribution in 2004 and confirms it appears on the CRTC’s authorized-for-distribution lists; it also notes the viral “Radio Act” framing is incorrect (and that Fox is available). No evidence located in these sources indicates the CRTC or courts cited a “Radio Act”/false-news prohibition as the reason Fox News is unavailable; instead, the available primary CRTC decision shows authorization, not denial or revocation, on those grounds.
evidence.scraped_text: CRTC concludes Fox News is not partially/totally competitive with Canadian services and "approves the requests ... to add Fox News ... to the digital lists"; it also declines to impose special conditions (unlike Al Jazeera) because nothing on the record gave sufficient cause for concern.

evidence.question: If Pelosi said something similar, what was the full context (event, topic, and complete remarks), and was she describing employers cutting hours as 'good' or discussing a different policy effect (e.g., ACA, part-time work, job flexibility)?
evidence.answer: No credible record shows Nancy Pelosi saying the viral quote about “Employers cutting hours” being “a good thing” so people can “pursue their dreams and passions.” The closest authentic, on-the-record remarks come from a CNN “State of the Union” interview on September 22, 2013 (Candy Crowley interviewing Pelosi) about the Affordable Care Act amid claims it would reduce work hours/shift workers to part-time. In that interview, Pelosi framed the ACA as a “liberation” and spoke broadly about “freedom to pursue your happiness,” including “follow your passion,” contrasting that with being “constrained by your policy” (i.e., job-lock due to health insurance). She was not praising employers cutting hours as “good”; she was arguing that access to health coverage outside a job increases personal freedom and wellness. A separate fact-check notes the specific “employers cutting hours is a good thing” wording has “no record” and appears to be a misrepresentation/splice of the 2013 CNN remarks.
evidence.scraped_text: In the ACA discussion, Pelosi says: “This is life, healthy life, liberty, the freedom to pursue your happiness which could be follow your passion for good rather than follow your palate and be constrained by your policy.” (Context: defending ACA; not a statement that employers cutting hours is ‘good.’)

evidence.question: In barrier-deployed areas, do official metrics show a decrease in illegal border crossings after barrier deployment compared with an appropriate pre-deployment baseline (controlling for broader sectorwide/nationwide trends)?
evidence.answer: The most directly relevant official, barrier-specific, before/after analysis with controls was not found in accessible federal sources during this check. A recent GAO report on 2017–2021 barrier construction focuses on cultural/natural-resource impacts and does not provide station/segment-level pre/post apprehension (or encounter) metrics, nor a controlled comparison against broader sector/nationwide trends; it only includes qualitative statements (e.g., that CBP officials noted reduced drug trafficking in some areas). CBP does publish official apprehension time series by Southwest Border sector (e.g., FY2024 monthly sector apprehensions), but these data are not tied to specific barrier deployment dates/locations and therefore cannot, on their own, establish a post-deployment decrease attributable to barriers versus broader policy/enforcement changes. Additionally, DHS itself cautions that apprehensions are a problematic proxy for illegal flows, underscoring the need for a more rigorous, controlled evaluation than what was located here.

evidence.answer: I did not find official, publicly available CBP/USBP data that isolates “barrier-deployed areas” and shows a before/after decrease in between-ports drug-trafficking indicators attributable to barrier deployment. DHS has publicly asserted that drug activity decreased in barrier areas and that barriers “force” smuggling toward ports of entry, but the example it provides is not a before/after, barrier-area time series; it instead cites increased seizures at the San Diego Field Office (ports of entry) from FY2019 to FY2020. Separately, non-government analysis drawing on CBP data indicates that fentanyl seizures declined after spring 2023 largely at ports of entry, while Border Patrol (between-ports/checkpoints) fentanyl seizures stayed roughly flat year-over-year in the cited months—suggesting that trends can differ by location type, but this still does not establish a barrier-area decrease. Overall, available evidence supports the general point that many hard-drug seizures occur at ports of entry and that POE trends can move differently than Border Patrol trends, but it is insufficient to verify a barrier-area post-deployment decrease using official data as framed in the question.

evidence.question: When was the online health consultation scheme launched, and does the timing align with the claim’s implication of a recent/announced launch (if any context suggests recency)?
evidence.answer: Evidence indicates India’s central-government online teleconsultation scheme (commonly referred to as the National Telemedicine Service “eSanjeevani”) was launched in two stages: the provider-to-provider (doctor-to-doctor) eSanjeevani AB-HWC variant launched in November 2019, and the direct patient-to-doctor “eSanjeevaniOPD” variant was rolled out on April 13, 2020 during the first COVID-19 lockdown. Therefore, if the claim “has launched an online health consultation scheme” is presented as a *new/recent* launch, that implication would be misleading because the launch dates are 2019/2020. If the claim is meant generically (i.e., the government has launched such a scheme at some point), it aligns with the historical launch timeline.

claim: The alleged concerns of the United States over Cuba's cooperation, in this case about the ‘More Doctors’ program, are neither legitimate nor pertinent to be discussed at PAHO [Pan American Health Organization].

evidence.question: Has the United States government publicly raised concerns about Cuba’s cooperation specifically in relation to the ‘More Doctors’ program (or Cuban medical missions) and, if so, what were the stated concerns and when were they made?
evidence.answer: Yes. The U.S. government (via the Secretary of State/State Department) has publicly raised concerns about Cuba’s overseas medical missions—including Brazil’s “Mais Médicos” (“More Doctors”) program—framing them as part of an “exploitative” Cuban labor export program involving forced labor/coercion and exploitation. Publicly stated concerns include: (1) that Cuba’s overseas medical missions are linked to forced labor/exploitation of Cuban health workers; (2) that the program enriches the Cuban government while withholding wages/benefits from workers; and (3) that it deprives ordinary Cubans of needed medical care at home. These concerns were publicly articulated at least by February 25, 2025 (visa-restrictions expansion announcement) and again in connection with actions targeting officials tied to the Mais Médicos program (reported August 13, 2025). Separately, U.S. congressional text also explicitly references Mais Médicos and alleges PAHO “facilitated” it, indicating the issue has been raised in official U.S. government documents tied to the program and PAHO’s role.
evidence.scraped_text: Dated Feb. 25, 2025: Rubio said expanded visa restrictions target Cuban officials tied to a labor program sending workers overseas, “particularly healthcare workers,” describing it as “forced labor.”

claim: During the Obama/Biden administration, 18,000 people got clemency.

evidence.question: What time period does the claim refer to by "Obama/Biden administration" (e.g., Jan 20, 2009–Jan 20, 2017), and is the clemency count intended to cover that full period?
evidence.answer: In common political usage, “Obama/Biden administration” refers to the period when Barack Obama was President and Joe Biden was Vice President—i.e., January 20, 2009 through January 20, 2017. The U.S. Department of Justice Office of the Pardon Attorney (OPA) explicitly labels “President Obama” as “January 20, 2009 - January 20, 2017” in its clemency statistics, indicating that clemency counts attributed to the “Obama Administration” are intended to cover that full presidential term. Separate OPA sections treat the “Biden Administration” as a different period (January 20, 2021 - January 20, 2025), so a claim phrased as “Obama/Biden administration” would most naturally map to the 2009–2017 Obama presidency (with Biden as VP), not Biden’s later presidency.

evidence.question: Does "clemency" in this claim mean only presidential grants of clemency (pardons and commutations), or does it include other forms (reprieves, amnesties, DOJ clemency initiative recommendations, state clemency, etc.)?
evidence.answer: In the federal context relevant to an “Obama/Biden administration” claim, authoritative DOJ materials define (or operationalize) executive clemency as actions taken under the President’s Article II power for federal offenses. DOJ’s clemency-processing regulations (28 C.F.R. Part 1) explicitly treat “executive clemency” as including pardon, reprieve, commutation of sentence, and remission of fine. The DOJ Justice Manual similarly describes petitions for “Executive Clemency” as including pardon, commutation, remission of fine or restitution, and reprieve. In practice, DOJ’s public “Clemency Recipients” listings and commonly cited clemency statistics for presidents are organized around presidential grants—especially pardons and commutations—rather than recommendations, initiatives, or state clemency. Therefore, for a numeric claim like “18,000 people got clemency” attributed to the Obama/Biden administration, the standard meaning is presidential grants (primarily pardons + commutations), not DOJ recommendations, state actions, or other non-presidential forms; though technically the federal executive clemency umbrella can also include reprieves and remissions.

claim: There was a 40% drop in oil prices in Nigeria in 2020.

evidence.question: What specific oil price metric is the claim referring to (e.g., Brent crude, Bonny Light, Nigeria’s official selling price, domestic pump price), and what were its values at the start and end (or peak-to-trough) of 2020?
evidence.answer: The phrase “oil prices in Nigeria” is ambiguous, but the most commonly referenced Nigeria-specific crude benchmark is Bonny Light. Evidence from OPEC-derived reporting indicates Bonny Light’s *annual average* price in 2020 was about $42.24/bbl versus $65.65/bbl in 2019 (a ~35.66% year-on-year drop), which is not 40%. Separately, OPEC-grade tables show Bonny Light averaged about $15.54/bbl in April 2020 and $24.86/bbl in May 2020, demonstrating a severe mid-2020 trough but not establishing a “40% drop” without specifying the comparison window (start-to-end 2020 vs peak-to-trough vs annual average). Because the claim does not specify the metric (Bonny Light vs Brent vs domestic pump price vs official selling price) or the comparison points, the “40% drop” cannot be verified as stated.

(1) Brent annual average fell from 2019 to 2020 by about 35% (not quite 40%): FRED (sourced from EIA) shows annual average Brent at $64.30 (2019) vs $41.96 (2020), a decline of ~34.7%. (2) Within 2020, Brent experienced a much larger peak-to-trough drop: EIA’s weekly Brent series shows ~$68.07/bbl (week of 2020-01-03) falling to ~$14.24/bbl (week of 2020-04-24), a drop of ~79%. (3) For Nigeria’s Bonny Light specifically (non-official aggregator source), monthly values show ~$58/bbl (2020-02-01) dropping to ~$16/bbl (2020-04-01), a drop of ~72%. Therefore, while the 2019-to-2020 annual-average decline is closer to ~35% than 40%, a ~40% (or greater) decline clearly occurred during 2020 on a peak-to-trough basis using EIA’s Brent series.

claim: US President Trump issued an executive order for the White House to take over all electrical grids, which will include internet servers, broadcasting systems and electronic systems

evidence.question: Did Donald Trump (as U.S. President) issue an executive order directing the White House (or Executive Office of the President) to take over all U.S. electrical grids?
evidence.answer: Searches of official executive order repositories and related coverage found no executive order in which President Donald Trump directs the White House/EOP to “take over all U.S. electrical grids.” The relevant Trump executive order located on this topic (April 8, 2025, “Strengthening the Reliability and Security of the United States Electric Grid”) directs actions by the Secretary of Energy under existing statutory emergency authority (Federal Power Act section 202(c)) and requires reserve-margin methodology and related processes; it does not order a White House takeover of the grid. Another related order (January 20, 2025, “Declaring a National Energy Emergency”) directs agencies to use lawful emergency authorities to facilitate energy development and mentions potential recommendations involving eminent domain or the Defense Production Act, but likewise does not direct the White House to seize or take over the nation’s electrical grids.
evidence.scraped_text: EO (Apr 8, 2025) focuses on grid reliability; directs the Secretary of Energy to streamline emergency orders under Federal Power Act 202(c) and develop/publish reserve margin methodology; no directive for the White House/EOP to take over the grid.

evidence.question: If any relevant executive order exists, does its text actually authorize a federal/White House takeover of electrical grids (e.g., nationalization, seizure, federal control), and what specific authorities and scope does it state?
evidence.answer: A relevant Trump executive order does exist: Executive Order 13920, “Securing the United States Bulk-Power System” (issued May 1, 2020). Its text does not authorize a federal/White House “takeover” (nationalization, seizure, or federal operation) of the U.S. electrical grid. Instead, EO 13920 declares a national emergency under IEEPA/NEA and prohibits certain transactions involving “bulk-power system electric equipment” when the Secretary of Energy determines the equipment is tied to a “foreign adversary” and poses specified risks. The order delegates implementation authority to the Secretary of Energy to stop or condition (license/mitigate) covered acquisitions/importations/transfers/installations and to issue implementing rules, create pre-qualified vendor/equipment criteria, and identify risky equipment for potential isolation/monitoring/replacement. The scope is focused on bulk-power system equipment and related procurement/supply-chain risk; it does not mention taking control of internet servers, broadcasting systems, or electronic systems generally, nor does it direct the White House to assume operational control of the grid.

evidence.question: Under U.S. law and existing emergency authorities, can the White House directly 'take over' electrical grids, internet servers, or broadcasting systems via executive order alone, and have any agencies (DOE, DHS/CISA, FERC, FCC) issued official statements or guidance indicating such a takeover occurred or was ordered?
evidence.answer: Available primary sources do not show any Trump executive order that directs the White House to “take over” all electrical grids, internet servers, broadcasting systems, or electronic systems. claim: A mail-in ballot application for voting in the US presidential election which was sent to a deceased grandfather in the State of New Mexico is evidence of potential widespread voter fraud via mail-in ballots.

evidence.question: Is there verifiable evidence (e.g., official statement, documented case report, credible news reporting) of a mail-in ballot application being sent in New Mexico to a specific deceased individual described as a 'deceased grandfather' in connection with a US presidential election?
evidence.answer: Yes. Credible reporting documents a 2020 social-media claim from New Mexico (Dona Ana County) stating a “grandfather” who died on December 31, 2014 received election mail. AFP Fact Check reports that the item shown was a *mail-in ballot application* (not a voted ballot) and attributes this clarification to Dona Ana County Clerk Amanda Lopez Askin. New Mexico’s 2020 special-session Senate Bill 4 text also confirms county clerks were authorized to automatically mail ballot applications to “mailable” voters ahead of the 2020 general election, consistent with the mechanism described in the AFP report.
evidence.scraped_text: AFP describes a Facebook post claiming: “My grandfather passed away December 31, 2014. We just got his ballot today,” and reports Dona Ana County Clerk Amanda Lopez Askin said the envelope was actually a mail-in ballot application that was automatically sent ahead of the 2020 general election.

evidence.question: Under New Mexico election procedures, can mail-in ballot applications be sent to outdated addresses or deceased individuals due to list maintenance, and what safeguards exist to prevent a ballot from being issued or counted in a deceased person's name?
evidence.answer: Yes. New Mexico’s procedures contemplate that voter rolls can temporarily contain outdated records (e.g., people who moved or recently died) until list-maintenance updates occur, which can result in election-related mail (including absentee/mailed-ballot materials) being sent to an address on file. New Mexico law provides for ongoing list maintenance using USPS change-of-address data and returned election mail, and separately requires monthly death-record matching: the state registrar files monthly certified lists of deceased residents with the Secretary of State, who forwards them to county clerks, and county clerks cancel the voter’s registration upon receipt. As safeguards against a ballot being issued or counted in a deceased person’s name: (1) a county clerk may only issue a mailed ballot if the applicant has a valid voter registration certificate on file; and (2) when a mailed ballot is returned, the county clerk must verify the voter’s signature on the outer envelope and confirm that the last four digits of the voter’s Social Security number match the information available to the clerk; if missing or not matching, the ballot is segregated and the voter must cure. These procedures mean that receiving an application or election mail addressed to a deceased person can reflect lag/maintenance issues rather than evidence that a ballot would be successfully issued and counted in that person’s name.
evidence.scraped_text: Requires monthly certified lists of deceased residents from the state registrar to the Secretary of State; SOS forwards lists to county clerks; county clerks cancel deceased residents’ voter registration certificates upon receipt.

evidence.question: What do official audits, investigations, prosecutions, or election administration reports show about the prevalence of mail-in ballot fraud in New Mexico and nationally in recent US presidential elections (e.g., 2016, 2020, 2024 if applicable)?
evidence.answer: Official and election-administration evidence located for New Mexico indicates that (a) unsolicited mailings to voters (including to deceased individuals) can occur as administrative mailings (e.g., absentee ballot *applications* sent broadly in 2020), and (b) New Mexico’s mail-ballot process requires a voter request/application before a ballot is issued—so an application sent to a deceased person is not, by itself, evidence that a ballot was cast fraudulently. Separately, available official reporting and prosecutions located in this search support the broader conclusion that proven election fraud—including fraud involving absentee/mail ballots—exists but is rare relative to total ballots cast, which does not support the claim of “widespread” mail-in ballot fraud. For example, a DOJ case describes an individual who voted by absentee ballot in 2020 using a stolen identity (a real but isolated criminal case), and a state election commission report in Wisconsin (as an example of election-administration reporting in a recent presidential election) shows suspected fraud/irregularity referrals were a tiny fraction of ballots cast (0.002% in that report).
evidence.scraped_text: AFP reports that a New Mexico county clerk said an envelope shown in a viral post was a mail-in ballot application (not a ballot), and that in 2020 some NM counties automatically sent applications to all voters due to COVID-era changes.

claim: The PHE has began to combine Covid-19 and influenza statistics to make the numbers look worse.

evidence.question: What does "PHE" refer to in this claim (e.g., Public Health England), and did that organization exist and publish COVID-19/influenza statistics during the relevant period?
evidence.answer: In the UK public-health context, “PHE” most commonly refers to Public Health England. GOV.UK’s official statistics page for “National flu and COVID-19 surveillance reports” explicitly lists the publisher as Public Health England and shows it was published starting 8 October 2020 (with updates through 19 August 2021), demonstrating PHE existed and published combined influenza/COVID-19 surveillance reporting during that period. Separately, UK government communications state that the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) became fully operational on 1 October 2021, and an official DHSC/PHE letter explains that from 1 October 2021 PHE’s health protection functions transferred into UKHSA prior to PHE’s formal closure—supporting the timeframe in which PHE was the responsible body for these surveillance publications (notably 2020–Aug 2021 on the cited statistics page).
evidence.scraped_text: Official Statistics page shows: “From: Public Health England”; “Published 8 October 2020”; “Last updated 19 August 2021”; and describes reports summarising surveillance of influenza and COVID-19.

evidence.question: Did PHE (or its successor body publishing the same surveillance outputs) ever publish combined COVID-19 and influenza statistics (e.g., a single metric aggregating both) rather than reporting them separately? If so, what exactly was combined and in which publications/dashboards?
evidence.answer: Evidence indicates PHE/UKHSA *combined the publications* (i.e., put influenza and COVID-19 surveillance into a single weekly report series), but did not publish a single aggregated metric that adds COVID-19 and influenza together. The GOV.UK ‘National flu and COVID-19 surveillance report’ format shows separate sections and separate indicators for influenza and COVID-19 (for example, separate positivity and hospital admission metrics). A contemporaneous fact-check (Full Fact, Oct 2020) also states the change meant publishing in one report, not adding the numbers together.
evidence.scraped_text: The report summarises surveillance used to monitor COVID-19, influenza, and other respiratory viruses, with separate ‘Influenza activity’ and ‘COVID-19 activity’ sections and separate laboratory metrics (e.g., influenza positivity vs COVID-19 PCR positivity) and separate hospital admission rates.

evidence.question: When (date/edition) did any change occur in PHE reporting that could be described as combining COVID-19 and influenza statistics, and what was the stated reason for the change?
evidence.answer: Public Health England (PHE) introduced a combined weekly publication covering both influenza and COVID-19 starting with the “National flu and COVID-19 surveillance report” first published on 8 October 2020 (week 41). GOV.UK’s description of the series states that the reports “summarise the surveillance of influenza, COVID-19 and other seasonal respiratory illnesses,” and explicitly says that “Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, for the 2020 to 2021 season the weekly reports will be published all year round.” This indicates a dated reporting change (launch of a combined flu+COVID surveillance series) with an official rationale tied to the COVID-19 pandemic and the need for year-round reporting during that season. The sources reviewed do not indicate that PHE merged flu and COVID counts into a single combined total; rather, they were reported together in one surveillance product.
evidence.scraped_text: Series first published 8 October 2020; describes reports summarising surveillance of “influenza, COVID-19 and other seasonal respiratory illnesses” and notes “Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, for the 2020 to 2021 season the weekly reports will be published all year round.”

evidence.answer: Available evidence indicates Public Health England (PHE) began publishing influenza and COVID-19 surveillance outputs in a single combined *report document* (starting 8 October 2020), while still presenting influenza and COVID-19 indicators separately; this change is described as a surveillance reporting integration/convenience measure, not as combining (adding) the diseases’ statistics to inflate totals.
